item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the heads-up as well as your kind words! I hadn't looked at this in a while, and goodness, I'm bad at remembering to check for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see a lingering 4000 that I assume got through edits (I would have changed it myself but with 4000 as a tag I wasn't 100% sure it wasn't intended.

Anyway loved it even if I'm confused. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is absolutely wonderful. It has a strong message, and it's executed extremely well.

However, I feel it could be expanded upon. We can see the origin of ANY SCP! An experiment log detailing the origin of some SCPs (173/96/683 might be risky to do, but i'd love to see it)

+1, and a suggestion to add an experiment log

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is so good idk why it's ranked so low. In my top 5 of all the 4000 proposals so far
+1 for sure

item.title: Update - A Little Actual History
item.content:encoded:
A Brief Monologue
Author Wibbling Ahead

After repeatedly banging my head against a wall to try and find any lingering problems with this work (metaphorically speaking, that is), I realized that the setup for Mr. Ericson's log was rather lacking. As such, he seemed like a bit of a misery-guts. Well, he kind of is, but not an interesting misery-guts. However, after agonizing of a way to fix this problem, a solution came to me:

I was focusing too much on one aspect of the story and not enough on the other.

'The Mysterious Teacher in the Suit' is half of the story, but its part wasn't there! I tell you, author blindness is a pestilence of mine. I'd looked over the article and looked over the article so many times, I was just thinking the ideas without actually writing them. A common mistake of mine, but one easily rectified.

Ta-da! Here is the Abridged Test Log Edit, which should address some of the problems with his 'arc', so to speak. Of course, the main problems I wished to tackle were brought to my attention through other perspectives, which (as I have recently learned) is my saving grace. Thank you very much to everyone who shared your opinions of my work with me! Even if something's imperfect, I only feel joy when it's mentioned because I know that I can fix it.

That said, I also appreciate anyone who had no problem with it before, though in a slightly different manner. As a side note, it tickles me to hear that emotion appears to be my strong suit. It's one thing to tell myself that I'm good at what I do, but it's quite another to hear it from someone else.

To sum up, whether you complemented or criticized, thank you very much for your words! Hopefully, the improvement is noticeable and, indeed, an improvement. Peace!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm torn.

On one hand, there is heart to this article. Lines like "I'm sorry, bud, but how do you expect the past to stay away from you when you won't stop living in it?" were particularly effective at bringing some emotion to the table.

On the other hand, the rest of the article is just so dry. You have a device that allows you to look into the origin of any object or person, with a witty and fun narrator to boot. The possibility there for fun examples, test logs, etc are exciting. But then you don't really do anything with it. Instead, the majority of the log on the researcher's descent is rather boring and riddled with cliches (Lover eaten by another SCP object, obsession over the loss of said lover, the inability of the Foundation to monitor and log what they are having a researcher do with an incredibly powerful piece of machinery and no follow up on this person's mental status during these tests, etc.)

Going to no vote. Interesting premise, but profoundly lacking on the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't put my finger on quite why I like this. I don't expect it's 4000 material, but I'm upvoting nonetheless. Got quite the stinger in that final line of dialogue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, and you secured my upvote already, but the description around the logs could be a little more clinical ("tears in his eyes" vs "he was distressed and unresponsive"). This is really well done especially considering it's a first article. Good luck in the contest!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to use this, but I'm also rather scared of it, which makes it effective in its nature as an SCP object. Definitely upvoted.

item.title: Well, shit.
item.content:encoded:
From the whole “machine that shows you the past” thing, I thought this was going to detail a journey through the history of humankind full of spooky shit like trickster gods and eldritch horrors… but instead, I was met with feelings, the past, and that line about staring being impolite. And honestly I think I like it more than what I was expecting.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
History may be the world's greatest teacher… but I'd avoid extra credit if I were you. Aren't conceptual hauntings just the worst?

At last! My grand debut! After all the time I've spent reading, I've finally decided to try adding something to this wonderful site! Here's to another legendary 10 years!

Also, innumerable thanks to Eskobar for his help in shaping the final product.
